items.hook: Christopher Nolan’s Odyssey used occupied land as a film set. That feels like a betrayal | Mohamed Sleiman Labat
items.body: The decision to shoot in Moroccan-occupied Western Sahara, where the Indigenous people can’t tell their stories without fear of imprisonment, helps erase our own brutal journey

items.hook: Robert Ervin Howard was an American author, known for writing pulp fiction in a diverse range of genres.
items.body: He created the character Conan the Barbarian and is regarded as the father of the sword and sorcery subgenre. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: The first banknotes in Europe are issued by the Swedish bank Stockholms Banco.
items.body: A banknote or bank note – also called a bill or simply a note – is a type of paper money that is made and distributed ("issued") by a bank of issue, payable to the bearer on demand. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.opener: Here's one for the group chat —
items.hook: Porte des Lilas is a 1957 French-Italian crime drama film directed by René Clair, based on René Fallet's novel La Grande ceinture.
items.body: The film is known as both Gates of Lilacs and The Gates of Paris, but was released under the latter title in the United States. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: Goodbye Chinatown by Kit Fan review – a chef’s elegy to London
items.body:
Skipping between London, Shanghai and Hong Kong, this tale of family migration, politics and food has plenty of flavour and fire

Amber Fan, the 22-year-old protagonist of Kit Fan’s heartfelt and elegiac second novel, is ready to sa

items.hook: KLM Flight 844 crashes off the Schouten Islands in present day Indonesia (then Netherlands New Guinea), killing 58 people.
items.body: KLM Flight 844 was an international scheduled passenger flight from Biak-Mokmer Airport, Dutch New Guinea to Manila International Airport, Manila, Philippines on 16 July 1957, which crashed into Cenderawasih Bay 1.2 kilometres from its departure airport. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: As many as 27 children die and 25 others are hospitalized after eating lunch served at their school in eastern India.
items.body: On 16 July 2013, 23–27 students died, and dozens more fell ill at a primary school in the village of Gandaman in the Saran district of the Indian state of Bihar after eating a Midday Meal contaminated with pesticide. Bring it up somewhere. Watch the reaction.

items.hook: King Richard II of England is crowned.
items.body: Richard II, also known as Richard of Bordeaux, was King of England from 1377 until he was deposed in 1399. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Actor Sam Neill died from pneumonia, agent confirms
items.body: Neill died in Sydney on Monday, with his family saying his death was 'sudden and unexpected'.

items.hook: The Invite review – A-list ensemble electrify hilarious couples night gone wrong comedy
items.body:
Sundance film festival: Olivia Wilde, Seth Rogen, Penelope Cruz and Edward Norton are exceptional in a smart and funny winner about sex, marriage and partner-swapping

Not enough people managed to see last year’s se

items.hook: Up All Night by Imogen Willetts review – a seductive history of going out
items.body:
From 18th-century pleasure gardens to Studio 54, the story of nightlife in all its hedonistic – and political – glory

In this fabulous alternative history of the modern world, the academic and “party historian” Imogen Willetts look

items.hook: Bellarion the Fortunate, published in 1926, is an historical novel by Rafael Sabatini.
items.body: Set at the beginning of the 15th century in Northern Italy, it takes place first in the Marquessate of Montferrat and later in the Duchy of Milan. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: The Luzon earthquake strikes the Philippines with an intensity of 7.7, affecting Benguet, Pangasinan, Nueva Ecija, La Union, Aurora, Bataan, Zambales and Tarlac.
items.body: The 1990 Luzon earthquake occurred on July 16 at 4:26 p.m. One of those things you can't un-know.

items.hook: Caught Stealing is a crime novel by American author Charlie Huston, published on May 31, 2005 by Ballantine Books.
items.body: The book is told in first-person narration by Henry “Hank” Thompson, a bartender in New York City who becomes embroiled in a struggle between several criminals. The world is stranger than it lets on.

items.hook: What if Bluey spoke one of the world's oldest living languages?
items.body: Five episodes of the award-winning children’s cartoon have been released in Australian Indigenous language for the first time.

items.hook: Four U.S. Marines and a United States Navy Sailor are killed in the a shooting spree targeting military installations in Chattanooga, Tennessee.
items.body: The United States Marine Corps (USMC), also referred to as the United States Marines or simply the Marines, is the naval infantry service branch of the United States Armed Forces. Worth a quiet trip down the rabbit hole.

items.hook: Jesy Nelson calls plan to test newborns for deadly muscle condition a 'victory'
items.body: All newborn babies are set to have a heel prick test for Spinal Muscular Atrophy (SMA) in England.
